subsystem: Ruby C extension: the Fiddle::Function#initialize entry point (a native method backing a Ruby class that wraps libffi). It takes a Ruby-supplied function pointer, an array of argument type codes, a return-type code, an ABI, and keyword options, and builds a libffi call-interface (ffi_cif) via ffi_prep_cif so Ruby can later call an arbitrary native function through this object.
provenance: Identified from the API surface: rb_scan_args, Check_Type/T_ARRAY, RARRAY_LEN/RARRAY_LENINT/RARRAY_AREF, rb_iv_set, rb_hash_foreach, TypedData_Get_Struct, INT2NUM/NUM2INT, xcalloc, and rb_raise/rb_eRuntimeError are all MRI (CRuby) internal C-extension APIs. The libffi symbols ffi_cif, ffi_type, ffi_status, ffi_prep_cif, FFI_DEFAULT_ABI, plus the helpers function_data_type, parse_keyword_arg_i, INT2FFI_TYPE and Check_Max_Args are specific to Ruby's ext/fiddle/function.c. The banner comment `sample: primevul_c_train_3143_vuln` marks it as a PrimeVul training sample; the filename tag cwe119 points at a memory-bounds class.
trust_boundary: This is a Ruby-callable native method: argc/argv (line 2) and therefore every scanned value — ptr, args, ret_type, abi, kwds (line 10) — are fully attacker-controllable from Ruby script. `args` is an arbitrary Ruby Array whose length and element objects the caller chooses (line 13-14). Critically, the array elements need not be plain Integers: NUM2INT on a non-Fixnum invokes the object's to_int coercion, i.e. arbitrary Ruby callback code can run mid-loop. The kwds hash (line 21) likewise drives a user-visible foreach callback. Everything downstream that indexes arg_types or feeds ffi_prep_cif inherits this hostile input.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the C implementation of a Ruby `initialize` method (the leading `static VALUE` was elided in the sample). `argc`/`argv` carry the Ruby call arguments and `self` is the receiver object being constructed. The locals declare a libffi call-interface pointer `cif`, a `ffi_type **arg_types` array-of-pointers that will describe each argument's type to libffi, an `ffi_status result` for the prep return code, five `VALUE`s to receive the parsed Ruby arguments, and a `long i` loop index. `arg_types` is the buffer at the center of this review; `i` is `long`, matching the `long` width of Ruby array lengths.

rb_scan_args destructures the Ruby argument list with the spec "31:": 3 required positional args (ptr, args, ret_type), 1 optional positional (abi), and a trailing `:` meaning a keyword/hash argument is collected into `kwds`. All five outputs are attacker-chosen Ruby objects. Line 11 supplies a default ABI (FFI_DEFAULT_ABI) when the caller omitted `abi`. No type validation happens here beyond arity.

Check_Type enforces that `args` is actually a Ruby Array (raising TypeError otherwise), so subsequent RARRAY_* calls are well-typed. Check_Max_Args reads the array length via RARRAY_LENINT (which returns an `int` and raises if the length overflows int) and validates it against Fiddle's maximum permitted argument count. This is a guard intended to keep the argument count bounded — but note it samples the length ONCE, here, and the value is not stored; the real allocation and fill loop below independently re-read RARRAY_LEN later.

The parsed values are stashed as Ruby instance variables on `self`. This also has the side effect of keeping these objects reachable by the GC for the lifetime of the Fiddle::Function. Storing the same `args` object that the loop below will iterate means the array remains the live, mutable object the caller still holds a reference to — it is not copied or frozen.

If keyword arguments were supplied, iterate the hash calling parse_keyword_arg_i for each pair to apply options to `self`. rb_hash_foreach runs the callback with attacker-controlled keys/values; the callback body is not shown here, so its side effects (and any Ruby code it may trigger) are a latent trust concern.

Retrieves the ffi_cif storage embedded in the wrapped `self` object (allocated by the type's alloc function per the `function_data_type` descriptor) and points `cif` at it. This is the destination that ffi_prep_cif will populate. TypedData_Get_Struct validates that `self` is of the expected data type.

Allocates the arg_types array with one slot per argument plus one trailing slot for a NULL terminator. RARRAY_LEN returns the array length as a `long`. xcalloc is Ruby's zero-initializing allocator that raises a Ruby exception on failure (it does not return NULL) and internally guards the count*size multiplication against overflow, so classic NULL-deref and calloc-multiply-overflow do not apply here. The security-critical fact is the sizing basis: the buffer is sized from RARRAY_LEN(args) as evaluated at THIS instant.

The loop walks each element of `args`, converting element `i` to a C `int` via NUM2INT(RARRAY_AREF(args, i)) and mapping that integer to a libffi type descriptor via INT2FFI_TYPE, stored at arg_types[i]. Line 31 writes the NULL terminator at index RARRAY_LEN(args). Crucially, the loop bound `RARRAY_LEN(args)` and the terminator index `RARRAY_LEN(args)` are RE-EVALUATED from the live array on every check, independently of the length used to size the buffer at line 25. NUM2INT on a non-immediate element invokes that object's to_int method (arbitrary Ruby), so the array's contents and length can change during iteration.

what_the_code_does.code:
result = ffi_prep_cif (
	    cif,
	    NUM2INT(abi),
	    RARRAY_LENINT(args),
	    INT2FFI_TYPE(NUM2INT(ret_type)),
	    arg_types);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Builds the libffi call interface: `cif` is the destination, `NUM2INT(abi)` selects the calling convention, `RARRAY_LENINT(args)` is the argument count (nargs), `INT2FFI_TYPE(NUM2INT(ret_type))` is the return type descriptor, and `arg_types` is the per-argument type array. Note the nargs passed to libffi is yet another fresh read of the array length (line 36), and the return type is derived from an attacker integer (line 37). ffi_prep_cif will iterate `nargs` entries of arg_types and dereference each ffi_type* to compute sizes/offsets.

If ffi_prep_cif returned a non-OK status (e.g. FFI_BAD_ABI or FFI_BAD_TYPEDEF), raise a RuntimeError including the numeric status. Otherwise the fully-initialized `self` (now holding a valid cif and the stored instance variables) is returned. Note that on the rb_raise path, `arg_types` (xcalloc'd at line 25) is leaked because there is no free before the non-local exit — a minor resource concern, not a memory-safety bug.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: TOCTOU heap out-of-bounds write. The buffer is sized once from RARRAY_LEN(args) at line 25, but the fill loop (line 27) re-reads RARRAY_LEN(args) as its bound on every iteration. Inside the loop, NUM2INT(RARRAY_AREF(args, i)) (line 28) will, for any element that is not a Fixnum, invoke that element's `to_int` method — arbitrary Ruby code executing while the C loop is mid-flight. If that callback appends elements to `args` (e.g. `args << 0`, or `args.concat(...)`), the loop's live bound RARRAY_LEN(args) grows, so the loop keeps iterating and writes `arg_types[i] = ...` at indices beyond the buffer that was allocated for the ORIGINAL, smaller length. This is a controlled heap buffer overflow writing attacker-influenced ffi_type* values past the end of a Ruby-heap allocation, corrupting adjacent heap metadata/objects; line 31 then writes NULL at the new, larger index, a further OOB write. The invariant the implementation must uphold: capture the array length into a single local BEFORE allocating, and use that same captured value for the allocation, the loop bound, the terminator index, and the ffi_prep_cif nargs — never re-read a mutable Ruby array length that a coercion callback can change between the size check and the write.

The maximum-argument-count guard is evaluated exactly once, at line 14, against the length at that instant, and the checked value is discarded rather than reused. Because the allocation (line 25), the fill loop (line 27), the terminator (line 31), and the nargs argument (line 36) each independently re-sample RARRAY_LEN(args), any growth of `args` after line 14 — again via a to_int callback during NUM2INT, or via a concurrent thread mutating the still-shared array — bypasses the cap entirely: libffi can be handed an nargs, and the loop can write, well beyond the vetted maximum. The check and the use must operate on the same captured length for the guard to mean anything.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
int type = NUM2INT(RARRAY_AREF(args, i));
	arg_types[i] = INT2FFI_TYPE(type);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Attacker-controlled type index into INT2FFI_TYPE. `type` is an arbitrary C int derived from a Ruby-supplied element. If INT2FFI_TYPE maps the integer through a lookup table without fully bounding it (or returns a descriptor for a partially-initialized type), an out-of-range code can yield an out-of-bounds read of the type table or an invalid/NULL ffi_type* that ffi_prep_cif (line 33) subsequently dereferences to read size/alignment — a crash or OOB read in libffi. The same hazard applies to the return type at line 37, INT2FFI_TYPE(NUM2INT(ret_type)). This is a latent finding: the safety depends on INT2FFI_TYPE's (unshown) implementation validating the integer and raising on unknown codes rather than indexing blindly.

The ABI is an unvalidated attacker integer passed straight to ffi_prep_cif. An out-of-range or unsupported ABI value should be rejected by libffi with FFI_BAD_ABI (surfaced as the RuntimeError at line 41), so under a correct libffi this is contained. The residual risk is only present if a platform's ffi_prep_cif does not defensively validate the abi enum; treat it as low severity and confirm the status is always checked (it is, at line 40).

Resource leak on the error path: when ffi_prep_cif fails, rb_raise performs a non-local exit without freeing the arg_types buffer allocated by xcalloc at line 25, leaking it once per failed initialization. An attacker who can reliably force ffi_prep_cif failure (e.g. a bad ABI/type) could repeatedly trigger this to grow memory. This is a robustness/DoS concern (CWE-401), not memory corruption, and is dominated by the TOCTOU finding above.

summary: A Ruby/libffi bridge whose central defect is a time-of-check/time-of-use heap overflow: the arg_types buffer is sized once from RARRAY_LEN(args) but the fill loop re-reads that length on every iteration, and NUM2INT on a non-Fixnum element runs attacker `to_int` code that can grow the array mid-loop, driving out-of-bounds writes past the allocation (and past the Check_Max_Args cap). Attacker-controlled type/ABI/return-type codes reaching libffi are a secondary, largely-contained concern.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: initialize(int argc, VALUE argv[], VALUE self) (line 2)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Ruby method entry signature; argc/argv are the untrusted source but carry no bound themselves.
The dynamically-sized destination buffer whose size basis diverges from its write bound — central to the TOCTOU overflow.
Loop index of correct (long) width matching RARRAY_LEN; no overflow issue on its own.
Arity/keyword destructuring only; introduces the untrusted VALUEs but performs no unsafe memory op.
Correctly enforces the array type; a proper gate, not a defect.
Caps the count once but the value is not captured/reused, so a later length re-read can bypass the cap.
Stores the reference (also GC-anchoring); no copy/freeze — contributes to mutability but is not itself the bug site.
User-driven callback, but runs before the allocation/loop and its body is not shown; no concrete overflow anchored here.
Type-checked retrieval of embedded cif storage; safe accessor.
Allocation length is sampled here but re-read elsewhere; the size/use divergence is the overflow root. (NULL-deref/multiply-overflow dismissed: xcalloc raises and guards the product.)
Loop bound re-evaluates a mutable length independent of the allocation size — the write that goes out of bounds.
Triggers to_int coercion (arbitrary Ruby) mid-loop, the mechanism that mutates args' length during iteration.
Maps an attacker integer to an ffi_type*; latent OOB/invalid-pointer risk if the code is not bounds-validated before libffi dereferences it.
Terminator index re-reads the mutable length; can write past the allocation after growth.
nargs is another fresh length read; feeds attacker abi/return-type into libffi. Overflow and unvalidated-enum surface.
Unvalidated ABI but libffi rejects bad values (status checked at line 40); low residual risk.
Non-local exit leaks the xcalloc'd arg_types buffer on the error path (resource leak / DoS).
Returns the initialized receiver; no security contract.

findings.title: TOCTOU heap out-of-bounds write: arg_types sized once, loop bound re-read while to_int callback grows args
findings.anchor: arg_types = xcalloc(RARRAY_LEN(args) + 1, ...); for (i = 0; i < RARRAY_LEN(args); i++) { ... arg_types[i] = INT2FFI_TYPE(type); }
findings.bug_class: Heap buffer overflow via time-of-check/time-of-use on a mutable length
findings.invariant: The Ruby array length must be read exactly once into a local before allocation, and that single captured value must be reused for the xcalloc size, the loop bound, the NULL-terminator index, and the ffi_prep_cif nargs; the array's length must not be allowed to change between sizing and filling.
findings.failure_mode: The caller passes an `args` array containing an object whose `to_int` appends elements (e.g. `evil.define_singleton_method(:to_int){ args << 0; 0 }`). xcalloc sizes the buffer for the original length N, but during the loop NUM2INT(RARRAY_AREF(args,i)) runs that callback, growing RARRAY_LEN(args). The loop's re-read bound now permits i >= N, so `arg_types[i] = INT2FFI_TYPE(type)` and later `arg_types[RARRAY_LEN(args)] = NULL` write pointer-sized values past the end of the heap allocation, corrupting adjacent heap data — a memory-corruption primitive from pure Ruby.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether the length is captured into a single local (e.g. `long len = RARRAY_LEN(args);`) and reused everywhere, or whether RARRAY_LEN(args)/RARRAY_LENINT(args) is re-evaluated at lines 25, 27, 31 and 36; and confirm NUM2INT can invoke user to_int on array elements.

findings.title: Max-argument cap is checked once and not reused, so a later length re-read bypasses it
findings.bug_class: Ineffective validation gate (check/use on divergent values)
findings.invariant: The value validated by Check_Max_Args must be the same captured length subsequently used to allocate and to bound the fill loop and nargs; growth between the check and the uses must be impossible.
findings.failure_mode: Because the checked length is discarded and every later use re-reads RARRAY_LEN(args), a to_int callback (or another thread sharing the still-mutable array) that grows `args` after line 14 defeats the cap: libffi and the loop then operate on a count exceeding the vetted maximum, compounding the overflow above and any downstream stack/type assumptions the cap was meant to protect.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the length flowing into Check_Max_Args is stored and reused for allocation/loop/nargs, and what limit Check_Max_Args enforces and why (libffi stack limits vs. buffer sizing).

findings.title: Attacker-controlled type codes mapped to ffi_type* without confirmed bounds validation
findings.bug_class: Improper validation of array index / invalid type pointer to libffi
findings.invariant: INT2FFI_TYPE must reject any integer that is not a recognized, fully-initialized ffi type code (raising a Ruby exception) before returning a descriptor, so no out-of-range table index or NULL/garbage ffi_type* reaches ffi_prep_cif.
findings.failure_mode: `type` (line 28) and `NUM2INT(ret_type)` (line 37) are arbitrary attacker integers. If INT2FFI_TYPE indexes a fixed type table without bounding the code, an out-of-range value causes an out-of-bounds read of the table or yields an invalid/NULL ffi_type* that ffi_prep_cif dereferences (reading size/alignment), producing a crash or OOB read inside libffi.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect the INT2FFI_TYPE macro/function: does it range-check the integer and raise on unknown codes, or index a table directly? Confirm the return-type path at line 37 shares the same validation.

findings.title: arg_types leaked on ffi_prep_cif failure (rb_raise non-local exit)
findings.invariant: Every allocation made by this function (the xcalloc at line 25) must be freed before any non-local exit, including the rb_raise error path.
findings.failure_mode: When ffi_prep_cif returns non-zero (reachable via attacker-chosen bad ABI or type), rb_raise unwinds without freeing arg_types, leaking one buffer per failed call; repeated forced failures cause unbounded memory growth (DoS).
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether arg_types is registered for cleanup or freed before rb_raise, or whether ffi_prep_cif failure simply leaks it.

audit_checklist: Is RARRAY_LEN(args) captured once into a local before line 25 and reused verbatim for the xcalloc size, the loop bound (line 27), the terminator index (line 31), and the ffi_prep_cif nargs (line 36)? Any re-read is the overflow root.
Can NUM2INT(RARRAY_AREF(args, i)) at line 28 invoke user-defined to_int, and can that callback mutate `args` (append/replace) during the loop? If so, the fill loop is exploitable.
Does Check_Max_Args validate the SAME length value later used, and what upper bound does it enforce and why?
Does INT2FFI_TYPE bounds-check the integer and raise on unknown/out-of-range codes before returning an ffi_type*, for both the arg loop (line 29) and the return type (line 37)?
Is arg_types freed on the rb_raise error path (line 41) and on any other early exit, and is `args` copied/frozen or left as the caller's live mutable object?
Does the code hold any Ruby object references correctly for GC while calling into libffi, given that user callbacks may run mid-initialization?
